quotedTweet.rawContent:
☢️You remember? Google optimized Shor's algorithm. The algorithm that breaks asymmetric cryptography (RSA, elliptic curves) once you have a quantum computer with enough Qubits.

The US government blocked the paper. So Google published a Zero Knowledge proof instead: a mathematical proof that they have the result, without revealing how. Cryptographic sorcery 🧙

But the Internet is sneaky. Someone launched a contest to re-discover the result with AI. The LLM searches a huge space of circuits (each one a candidate optimization of Shor's), and tests whether it beats the previous best. The clever part: they use the ZKP verifier as the reward function. No false positives, and it turns out to be a very efficient signal.

In less than 2 days, the community re-discovered Google's result !!!

🔔15 days later, the LLMs are still improving it. They're already 44% ahead of Google.

Hard to say where this stops, ie. what the true minimum quantum complexity for Shor's is. But we will not close the full gap. You still need a Quantum Computer with a relatively large number of qubits. The only thing that changed is that this number drops a little every day, and it has been dropping for 15 days straight.

BYD, Google, AMD, Tesla and others are looking increasingly to Samsung Electronics for contract chipmaking services as surging demand for AI infrastructure strains the advanced chipmaking capacity of market leader TSMC, Nikkei Asia has learned.

Samsung has seen a surge in inquiries from existing and potential global customers, including those in China, looking to use its advanced chip manufacturing capacity, according to six people with knowledge of the matter.

BYD, China's top electric vehicle maker, has been in talks with Samsung about building future generations of autonomous driving chips, while Google is engaging with the South Korean company about potentially producing its next-generation Axion processors, set to launch around 2028, two people familiar with the matter told Nikkei Asia. Google is also considering asking Samsung to build part of its key Tensor Processing Units (TPUs) for AI computing workloads, as early as 2028, one of the people said.

"TSMC is prioritizing advanced-node production not only because it reinforces its tech leadership and long-term strategy, but also because those nodes are more profitable and remain in short supply," said an executive from a Chinese autochip designer, who asked not to be named. "Samsung's yields still lag behind TSMC's, but capacity availability has made it an increasingly attractive option."

Yield is the industry term for production quality, measured as the percentage of chips produced that meet the required specifications and performance.

TSMC, Samsung and Intel are the world's only chipmakers that can offer cutting-edge chip production at a large scale. TSMC has secured the majority of such orders, as well as orders for advanced chip packaging services, from the likes of Nvidia, Apple, AMD, Broadcom, Marvell and MediaTek. These clients take up a large share of the Taiwanese chipmaking group's advanced chipmaking capacity.

Intel, which mainly manufactures chips for in-house use, is working to attract more customers to adopt its advanced chipmaking and packaging capacity.

The Chinese auto industry executive said many chip designers in the country are now pursuing a dual-sourcing strategy, spreading orders between TSMC and Samsung rather than relying on a single foundry, with exact allocation depending on the product.

"Geopolitical factors also push some U.S. clients to use multiple contract chipmakers, if available out there, if they can," another chip industry executive with knowledge told Nikkei Asia.

"TSMC's advanced chip production capacity is really full, which is hard for Chinese clients who have smaller volumes to place new orders, so several have turned to approach Samsung for future possibilities," said an executive-level chip industry source who is familiar with the matter.

Google has been a driving force in the multi-foundry strategy, sources told Nikkei Asia. MediaTek, Taiwan's top chip developer, is helping Google to work with both TSMC and Intel for advanced chip packaging, Nikkei Asia earlier reported, while Qualcomm, the U.S. mobile chip king, has long made use of both TSMC's and Samsung's services.

However, using multiple foundries for advanced chip production requires substantial investment in adapting chip designs to different chipmakers' manufacturing processes, along with greater R&D and supply-chain coordination. Such a strategy is typically viable only for deep-pocketed chip developers, given the additional engineering and workforce costs involved.

Meanwhile, capacity constraints at TSMC are pushing other existing customers to potentially place more orders with Samsung, sources said.

Tesla works with both Samsung and TSMC on various products, including its AI5 chips used in its cars and robots. The upcoming AI6, however, will be made by Samsung in Texas. Tesla founder Elon Musk also announced a partnership with Intel for its future terafab projects in Texas, aiming to bring more critical chip production back to the U.S. and help meet growing demand.

AMD and Nvidia, key clients of TSMC, are also working with Samsung on some chip projects due to capacity constraints. AMD, for example, is in talks to build some future CPUs with Samsung from 2028, one source with knowledge of the situation said.

Nvidia's Groq language processing units (LPUs) are produced by Samsung, and the U.S. chipmaker has not decided whether production of the next generation of these highly specialized chips will be given to other contract chipmakers. The majority of Nvidia's chips are made exclusively by TSMC.

The increasing requests for leading-edge chip production for Samsung would mark a victory for the South Korean chipmaker's foundry business, which used to mainly serve its own consumer electronics and home appliance divisions.

The growing foundry business comes as Samsung is also riding the AI investment boom, enjoying exponentially growing demand for its memory products, including high-bandwidth memory chips, DRAM and NAND flash memories.

Samsung said that it does not comment on customers.

New orders would help Samsung's foundry unit rebound from a recent downturn. The company said earnings for the segment declined in the first quarter due to it being the off-peak season, but it did not elaborate on how big the dip was.

Samsung's foundry and system large-scale integration business units posted a combined 6.9 trillion won ($4.6 billion) in sales in the first quarter, up from 6 trillion won a year ago. Samsung does not break down its earnings for the two units, the latter of which makes an array of logic and analog chips, including image sensors and display chips.

BYD declined to comment. Google said it does not comment on the details of its supply chain.

My talk "SELECT * FROM binary - Vibe Reversing Across IDA, Ghidra, and Binary Ninja" got accepted at @reconmtl . 

I am happy to talk about this toolset (idasql, bnsql and ghidrasql) and how it is designed to allow AI agents to work across all these top 3 reverse engineering tools. 

Point Claude, Codex, or your favorite agent to any single one or all of them at once, and allow your agent can now use the tools all at once to analyze one or more binary at once.

These work nicely with loops like "/goal" to allow e2e analysis over many hours.

Use cases:
- Full source code recovery (with types and code structure)
- Full database annotations (also with type recovery, comments, etc.)
- Binary diffing
- Or just any kind of Q&A against your binary or binaries

https://t.co/ohevK9zzxn

RT @FuzzingLabs: 🛰️ We broke RTKLIB, the open-source engine behind centimeter-accurate GPS in drones, boats, autonomous vehicles & survey networks.

4 memory-corruption bugs in its RTCM3 / RINEX decoders. No auth. Reachable from a single crafted correction stream or one booby-trapped file.

1 OOB write + 3 OOB reads. A rogue NTRIP caster or a MITM'd stream is enough to crash or corrupt the positioning stack of a machine that moves itself.

Reported upstream, coordinated disclosure (RTKLIB #796–799).

Full write-up 👇

Researchers reverse engineered BattlEye's entire client protocol and emulated it from scratch.

The result: playing BattlEye-protected games online without BattlEye even installed. Live debugging the game while connected to real servers. Cheat Engine running in Escape from Tarkov. All because the packet encryption was static and contextless.

Every layer gets reverse engineered. The arms race never stops.

tweets.content:
RT @SynackRedTeam: Dead-end injection points aren't dead ends if you know where to look.

In our latest Exploits Explained, Synack Red Team researcher @ozgur_bbh walks through a fully blind SQL injection buried inside a PostgreSQL ORDER BY clause, where boolean chaining, time-based techniques, and UNION attacks were all off the table.

His solution? Recognize that regex functions trigger errors PostgreSQL doesn't catch during query planning, isolate them inside a subquery so they only fire on the false branch of a CASE statement, and use the error/success difference as a boolean oracle to extract data character by character.

Check out the full post to learn more 👉 https://t.co/1xtO9d3uKa

tweets.content: RT @ImposeCost: Something I've tweeted about in the past but that is coming to mind as I look at people being concerned with "burn out" is how often it is misattributed to fatigue from overworking. While chronic overworking can be a problem, I believe most issues with "burn out" stem from degrading morale within an organization. A key way to tell is if you take a bunch of time off, come back to work, and instantly get the feeling back. You're not fatigued; you're disillusioned with your current situation.

tweets.content:
RT @OSdev_: One of the most interesting privilege escalation bugs in the Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) is CVE-2018-0743 - https://t.co/KvttafUnyB

The vulnerability stemmed from improper handling of objects in memory within WSL. By exploiting this flaw, a local authenticated attacker could manipulate the way Windows managed these objects, allowing them to elevate their privileges beyond their intended level. The resulting access could ultimately grant the attacker full administrative (SYSTEM-level) privileges, enabling complete control over the affected machine.

Microsoft addressed the issue as part of its January 2018 security updates, eliminating the underlying memory handling flaw and preventing attackers from leveraging it for privilege escalation.

tweets.content:
RT @OSdev_: One of the most interesting Windows kernel bugs is CVE-2021-36934, better known as HiveNightmare.
 https://t.co/fIVVseC1rx

The issue wasn't memory corruption or a race condition, it was a simple ACL misconfiguration. The "SAM", "SYSTEM", and "SECURITY" registry hive backups inside "C:\Windows\System32\config\RegBack" could be read by low-privileged users, exposing password hashes and secrets that should never leave the kernel's trust boundary.
